MU.headline: Two-quarter beat-and-raise cadence accelerating — Q2 revenue beat guide by 28%, EPS by 45%; Q3 guide implies further +40% QoQ.

shifts.note: QoQ growth re-accelerated from +21% to +75% and is guided to +40% again
shifts.trend: Revenue $13.6B → $23.9B actual → $33.5B guide (YoY: +57% → +196% → implied +250%+)

shifts.note: Pricing power compounding — AI demand outpaces industry supply
shifts.trend: Non-GAAP GM 56.8% → 74.9% → ~81% guide (≈ +24 pts of expansion in two quarters)

shifts.note: Beat magnitude widening, not narrowing — credibility on the raised Q3 guide is high
shifts.trend: Q2 revenue guided $18.7B → printed $23.9B (+28% beat); Q3 now guided to $33.5B (+40% QoQ)

shifts.note: Same constraint language for two consecutive quarters; tightness is persistent, not transient
shifts.trend: 'Supply allocation decisions may impact certain customers' (Q1) → 'Industry supply allocation decisions continue' (Q2) — no easing language added

shifts.note: HBM hyperscaler reliance increasing — single-customer dependency to monitor
shifts.trend: One customer reached 13% of six-month revenue (Q2 disclosure) — primarily CMBU (HBM/hyperscale)

shifts.note: Singapore HBM advanced-packaging fab broke ground; capacity additions from CY2027
shifts.trend: FY26 capex ~$20B (Q1 guide); Q1 capex $5.39B + Q2 capex $5.0B = $10.4B run-rate H1 — on pace for $20B+

shifts.note: First meaningful dividend hike in years; mgmt signaling cycle durability
shifts.trend: Dividend $0.115/share (Q1) → $0.15/share (Q2, +30%)

MU.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening — beat-and-raise cadence is widening, gross-margin expansion is compounding, and guidance credibility is high. The only emerging risk is HBM customer concentration (one customer at 13% of revenue). Supply tightness language has not eased — bullish for memory pricing through CY2026.

NVDA.headline: DC revenue held +66–68% YoY through FY26 with Blackwell majority; Q1 FY27 guided $43B implies sequential step-down absorbed by GM stability at 73.5%.

shifts.trend: DC +66% YoY (Q3 FY26) → +68% (FY26 full year); revenue $57.0B Q3 annualized → FY26 10-K full-year record
shifts.note: Blackwell ramp sustained acceleration through year-end

shifts.trend: Q3: 4 customers each >10% (22/15/13/11%); FY26 10-K: hyperscaler share maintained; no new customer below 10% threshold
shifts.note: Hyperscaler dependency newly disclosed and persistent

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 China $3.0B (vs $8.1B prior-year Q3) → H20 effectively zero post-$4.5B Q1 FY27 charge; China optionality fully impaired
shifts.note: Geopolitical risk fully realized — model China at zero going forward

shifts.trend: Non-GAAP GM 73.4% (Q3 FY26) → 73.5% guided Q1 FY27; Blackwell full-system mix compressed vs H100 peak but holding range
Both Q3 and FY26 10-K cite DC energy/capital availability as gating constraint; $7.5B new DC lease obligations added Q4 FY26; $17.5B private infra investments disclosed
shifts.note: Supply constraint language unchanged across three consecutive filings

shifts.trend: FY26 10-K: $17.5B private investments + $3.5B land/power guarantees; Q3 FY26: $7.5B new DC lease obligations FY26-FY30
shifts.note: Vendor-financing footprint growing — watch FCF conversion

NVDA.verdict: Thesis intact: DC supply-constrained demand compounding into GB300 cycle; China permanently impaired is the key model reset; $43B Q1 FY27 guide at 73.5% GM leaves the investment case unchanged.

AMD.headline: DC segment re-accelerated from +32% to +57% YoY in one quarter while DC operating margins expanded 600bps to 28% — China MI325 license reopens a previously closed door.

shifts.trend: Revenue: +34% FY25 → +38% Q1 FY26; DC: +32% FY25 → +57% Q1 FY26; DC revenue $16.6B FY25 → $5.8B in one quarter ($23B+ annualized run-rate)
shifts.note: DC acceleration is the single most important data point in this window

shifts.trend: DC op margin 22% FY25 (dragged by $440M MI308 China charges) → 28% Q1 FY26; overall GM 50% → 53% (+3pts QoQ)
shifts.note: Charge-free quarter reveals true DC margin potential

shifts.trend: FY25: $440M MI308 write-down on China export ban; Q1 FY26: MI325 licenses granted Feb 2026 with 25% tariff — China TAM partially restored
shifts.note: Tariff headwind remains but loss of market access reversed

shifts.trend: No single customer >10% disclosed in either filing; DC growth broad-based across cloud deployments
Q1 FY26 DC $5.8B beat implied run-rate; management cited continued strong MI300/MI325 demand with no inventory digestion signals
DC $16.6B FY25 → $5.8B Q1 FY26; Client/Gaming mix declining as revenue mix shifts toward higher-margin DC
shifts.note: Revenue quality improving alongside quantity

AMD.verdict: Thesis strengthened: DC re-acceleration to +57% with margin expansion to 28% and China license restoration removes the two largest bear cases simultaneously — AMD is closing the gap to NVDA faster than consensus expected.

AMAT.headline: DRAM/HBM mix shift accelerating — DRAM jumped from 26% to 34% of SemiSys in one quarter while China exposure compressed and Taiwan surged +46%; BIS settlement removes the overhang.

shifts.trend: DRAM mix in SemiSys: 26% FY25 → 34% Q1 FY26; foundry/logic 67% → declined as HBM investment front-loads WFE
shifts.note: Memory mix shift is the key signal for HBM-driven WFE spending

shifts.trend: China revenue $8.5B FY25 (-16% YoY) → $2.1B Q1 FY26 (-7% YoY); China share of mix compressing as Taiwan/Korea fill gap
shifts.note: Structural China derisking continuing — partially offset by allied-nation wins

shifts.trend: Revenue +4% FY25 → -2% YoY Q1 FY26; Taiwan +46% offset by China pressure; Q2 FY26 DRAM/HBM tailwind expected to re-accelerate
shifts.note: YoY optics weak but mix improving — DRAM recovery is the inflection

shifts.trend: GM 48.7% FY25 → 49.0% Q1 FY26 (+30bps); $253M BIS settlement charge absorbed without structural margin damage
Two customers at 19% and 15% FY25; Taiwan concentration rising as share +46%; Korea growing on HBM capacity adds
$253M BIS settlement paid Q1 FY26 — compliance overhang resolved; no new export restrictions disclosed in Q2 FY26 filing
shifts.note: Settlement removes the largest near-term risk to China operations

AMAT.verdict: Thesis intact: DRAM/HBM mix shift is the leading indicator for AMAT's next up-cycle; BIS settlement clears the regulatory cloud; watch Q2 FY26 for confirmation that the DRAM ramp accelerates past the weak Q1 YoY print.

CSCO.covers: Q1 FY26 → Q2 FY26 → Q3 FY26
CSCO.headline: Networking growth accelerated from +15% to +21% YoY across two quarters driven by Silicon One AI fabric demand; Security drag from Splunk transition is the offsetting headwind.

shifts.trend: Total revenue +8% Q1 FY26 → +10% Q2 FY26; Networking +15% Q1 → +21% Q2; Silicon One inventory/commitments +9% signaling forward demand
shifts.note: Networking re-acceleration is AI-driven and broadening

shifts.trend: Networking: +15% → +21% YoY; Security: -4% Q2 on Splunk model shift; US geography +13% Q1 reflecting hyperscaler pulls
shifts.note: Portfolio diverging — AI networking strong, security transitional

shifts.trend: GM improving QoQ as Silicon One volumes scale; Splunk integration costs declining through Q2-Q3
Silicon One commitments +9% QoQ in Q1 — supplier lock-in signals backlog building; Q3 continuation of Networking momentum expected
shifts.note: Forward commitments are a leading indicator of sustained demand

shifts.trend: US at +13% Q1; no single customer >10% disclosed; hyperscaler Ethernet share gains broad-based
Q1 guide met; Q2 beat; Q3 FY26 filed May 2026 — three consecutive quarters of Networking outperformance vs consensus
shifts.note: Guidance cadence building credibility on AI networking thesis

CSCO.verdict: Thesis intact: Silicon One/Ethernet AI fabric is taking real share in a growing market; Security drag is transitional not structural; three-quarter Networking acceleration pattern validates the AI networking spend thesis.

META.headline: Revenue growth re-accelerated to +33% QoQ while capex guidance was raised again mid-quarter — FY26 infra spend now $125–145B, up from $115–135B just 90 days earlier.

shifts.trend: Revenue +22% FY25 → +33% Q1 2026; advertising ARPU expanding as Llama/AI recommendation engines lift engagement
shifts.note: Re-acceleration at $200B+ revenue base is exceptional

shifts.trend: FY26 capex guided $115–135B (Jan 2026) → raised to $125–145B (Apr 2026) → April added $24B more infra contracts mid-quarter; non-cancelable commitments $237.7B
shifts.note: Capex raise of $10–20B in 90 days signals hyperscaler arms race intensifying

shifts.trend: Reality Labs op loss $19.2B FY25 → $4.03B Q1 2026 alone (annualizing $16B+); RL loss trajectory not improving
shifts.note: RL is a persistent cash drain on an otherwise excellent business

shifts.trend: $29.9B bond issuance Nov 2025; non-cancelable commitments $237.7B Q1 2026; balance sheet leverage increasing to fund AI infra build
Core advertising margins strong; effective tax rate 30% FY25 → guided 13–16% FY26 — tax tailwind adds ~$3–4B to net income
shifts.note: Tax rate normalization is a material earnings unlock in 2026

shifts.trend: FY26 capex $115–135B → $125–145B → April $24B incremental = potential $149B+ total; pattern of raising guidance within quarter
shifts.note: Mid-quarter raise is unusual and signals demand visibility exceeds 90-day horizon

META.verdict: Thesis strongly intact: +33% revenue growth with tax tailwind, rising AI engagement, and a management team willing to raise capex mid-quarter signals they see demand well ahead of consensus; RL losses are the only structural drag.

MSFT.headline: Azure accelerated from +39% to +40% YoY in consecutive quarters while RPO held at $625–627B; capex $29.9B → $30.9B with 9-month total of $80.1B vs $40.9B prior year.

shifts.trend: Azure: +39% Q2 FY26 → +40% Q3 FY26; Cloud segment: $51.5B → $54.5B (+6% QoQ); total revenue +17% → +18%
shifts.note: Sustained acceleration at $220B+ annualized Cloud run-rate is the key signal

shifts.trend: Quarterly capex $29.9B Q2 → $30.9B Q3; 9-month total $80.1B vs $40.9B prior year (+96%); full-year FY26 tracking ~$115B+
shifts.note: Capex nearly doubled YoY — capacity additions will pressure near-term FCF

shifts.trend: Cloud GM fell QoQ as AI infra ramp dilutes margin mix; $5.9B OpenAI recapitalization gain in Q3 flatters reported net income
shifts.note: Underlying cloud margin compression is masked by one-time OpenAI gain

shifts.trend: RPO $625B Q2 → $627B Q3 (flat QoQ despite record consumption); backlog replenishment matching draw-down — demand not slowing
RPO $625B → $627B QoQ; no step-down in committed revenue despite high Q3 consumption; forward pipeline intact
Azure AI services embedded across enterprise and hyperscaler; no single customer concentration risk disclosed; diversification growing

MSFT.verdict: Thesis strongly intact: Azure acceleration into Q3 with stable RPO and doubling capex is the clearest evidence of sustained AI infrastructure demand — cloud GM pressure is the only near-term friction worth monitoring.

AMZN.headline: AWS grew from +20% to +28% YoY and RPO surged from $244B to $364B in one quarter on a $100B OpenAI expansion — capex $43.2B in Q1 alone (+78% YoY).

shifts.trend: AWS YoY: +20% FY25 → +28% Q1 2026; AWS quarterly run-rate $128.7B → $37.6B ($150B+ annualized)
shifts.note: AWS re-acceleration is the clearest demand signal in hyperscaler reporting

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $128.3B (+65% YoY) → Q1 2026 capex $43.2B (+78% YoY); FY26 total expected to exceed FY25
shifts.note: Capex intensity at 78% YoY growth is the highest among hyperscalers this quarter

shifts.trend: RPO: $244B FY25 → $364B Q1 2026 (+49% in one quarter); $100B OpenAI single expansion drove the jump; $50B committed to OpenAI total
shifts.note: RPO surge of $120B in 90 days has no precedent in AWS history

shifts.trend: OpenAI $50B total commitment + Anthropic $25B commitment = $75B in two AI customers; concentration risk elevated
shifts.note: AI lab customer concentration is a new risk to monitor

shifts.trend: AWS op margin 39.4% FY25 → 37.7% Q1 2026 (-170bps); capex front-loading diluting near-term margin
shifts.note: Margin compression is intentional and temporary — capacity for future growth

shifts.trend: FY26 capex guided to increase beyond $128.3B FY25; Q1 capex already $43.2B — full-year trajectory toward $160–180B

AMZN.verdict: Thesis strongly intact: AWS re-acceleration to +28% with $120B RPO surge in one quarter is the most bullish datapoint in the cloud sector; margin compression is deliberate capacity investment, not demand weakness.

GOOGL.headline: Google Cloud accelerated from +36% to +63% YoY in one quarter while RPO nearly doubled from $242.8B to $467.6B — TPU multi-GW supply agreements signal infrastructure-scale AI commitment.

shifts.trend: Cloud revenue +36% FY25 → +63% Q1 2026; cloud op income $13.9B FY25 → implied $26B+ annualized; cloud op margin 33% Q1 (3x YoY improvement)
shifts.note: The +63% re-acceleration on $20B quarterly base is exceptional

shifts.trend: RPO $242.8B FY25 → $467.6B Q1 2026 (near-doubled in one quarter); 'multi-GW' TPU supply agreements newly disclosed — infrastructure lock-in
shifts.note: Multi-GW TPU agreements signal a new class of infrastructure commitment

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $91.4B (+74% YoY); Q1 2026 capex $35.7B; FY26 guided to 'significantly increase' — full-year tracking $130B+
Cloud op margin: implied <5% FY24 → scaled to 33% Q1 2026; Wiz + Intersect acquisitions add $35B deployed capital but expand TAM
shifts.note: Cloud margin expansion from near-zero to 33% validates the Cloud scale thesis

shifts.trend: $40B private company investments disclosed Q1 2026; no single AI customer dominates cloud revenue unlike AWS/OpenAI concentration
FY25 $91.4B (+74%) → Q1 alone $35.7B; Wiz $29.5B + Intersect $5.9B closed; M&A capex accelerating alongside organic infra
shifts.note: M&A + organic capex together signal most aggressive Google Cloud push ever

GOOGL.verdict: Thesis strongly intact and accelerating: Cloud at +63% with 3x margin expansion and RPO near-doubling is a historic inflection — the TPU multi-GW agreements suggest Google is matching hyperscaler-level AI infrastructure commitments that competitors can't easily replicate.

ORCL.headline: OCI accelerated from +68% to +84% YoY while RPO expanded from $523B to $552B and 9-month capex ran $39.2B — a 3.2x increase over prior year driving operating margin compression.

shifts.trend: OCI: +68% Q2 FY26 → +84% Q3 FY26; total revenue +14% Q2 → +22% Q3; OCI quarterly revenue $4.08B → $4.89B (+20% QoQ)
shifts.note: OCI acceleration to +84% is the fastest cloud growth of any major provider this cycle

shifts.trend: RPO $97.3B → $523.3B Q2 → $552.6B Q3; RPO has grown 5x YoY — backlog building faster than consumption
shifts.note: RPO at 8x annualized revenue is extreme — signals years of committed demand

shifts.trend: H1 capex $20.5B vs $6.3B prior year (3.3x); 9M capex $39.2B vs $12.3B prior (3.2x); full-year tracking $50B+ vs ~$16B FY25
9M op margin declining as infra ramp dilutes; $47.7B total bonds outstanding vs $17.9B at Q2; debt-funded capex compressing FCF
shifts.note: Margin sacrifice is intentional — capacity for RPO $552B backlog

shifts.trend: $17.9B bonds Q2 → $47.7B Q3; Ampere stake sold $4.3B Q2 to partially fund infra; balance sheet leverage rising significantly
shifts.note: Debt-funded growth is the key risk — watch interest coverage

shifts.trend: OCI +68% → +84% sequential acceleration with no slowdown signaled; RPO $552.6B implies multi-year revenue visibility at current draw-down rates

ORCL.verdict: Thesis intact but leverage is the critical watch: OCI at +84% with $552B RPO is a demand story that rivals AWS/Azure; however $47.7B in bonds funding a 3x capex ramp requires interest coverage discipline — the investment case hinges on OCI reaching scale before debt service becomes a constraint.

MRVL.headline: DC mix reached 74% of revenue at +46% YoY for the full year while GM expanded from 41.3% to 51.0% — Celestial AI and XConn acquisitions signal a pivot toward optical interconnect for AI clusters.

shifts.trend: DC revenue +38% Q3 FY26 → +46% full-year FY26; total revenue +37% Q3 → +42% FY26; DC share 73% Q3 → 74% FY26
shifts.note: DC concentration accelerating — MRVL is increasingly a pure-play DC networking/ASIC company

shifts.trend: GM 23% Q3 FY25 (impaired by $357M write-downs) → 51.6% Q3 FY26 → 51.0% FY26 full year; sustainable margin floor now established
shifts.note: True margin power visible once prior-year impairments excluded

shifts.trend: Distributor A 38% Q3 → 37% FY26; Customer A 14% FY26 (new disclosure); top-customer concentration elevated and rising
shifts.note: Single-distributor dependency at 37% is the key risk to monitor

shifts.trend: Segment consolidation to DC + Comms; DC $1.52B Q3 → $6.1B FY26 ($24B+ annualized run-rate); Comms declining as DC dominates
Celestial AI acquired Feb 2026 (~$1.3B cash + 24.5M shares, $5.5B value); XConn acquired ($280M); M&A capex accelerating into optical interconnect
shifts.note: Acquisitions signal MRVL is building an end-to-end AI interconnect stack

shifts.trend: China 40% of Q3 revenue via Distributor A; FY26 10-K no explicit China breakdown — opacity increasing as geopolitical risk rises
shifts.note: 40% China exposure is the single largest thesis risk

MRVL.verdict: Thesis intact with concentration risk elevated: MRVL's DC ASIC and optical interconnect pivot is confirmed by 74% DC mix and 51% GM; Distributor A at 37% and 40% China exposure are the two tail risks that could break the thesis on a policy shock.

AVGO.headline: Semiconductor revenue accelerated from +24% to +52% YoY in one quarter as top-5 customer concentration rose from ~40% to ~50% — custom ASIC AI demand is outpacing the software segment.

shifts.trend: Semi revenue: +24% FY25 → +52% Q1 FY26; total revenue +24% FY25 → +29% Q1 FY26; software +1% Q1 vs semi +52% — portfolio diverging sharply
shifts.note: Semi acceleration to +52% is custom AI ASIC (Google TPU, Meta MTIA) driven

shifts.trend: Top-5 end customers ~40% FY25 → ~50% Q1 FY26; distributor share 32% → 42%; hyperscaler ASIC concentration rising
shifts.note: 10-point concentration increase in one quarter is a material risk disclosure

shifts.trend: GM 68% FY25 → 68% Q1 FY26 (flat); op margin 44% Q1; software-semi mix shift not yet impacting blended GM
Semi $36.9B FY25 → $12.5B Q1 FY26 ($50B annualized); software $27.0B → $6.8B ($27B flat); semi closing the gap to software in revenue contribution
China 17% FY25; Q1 FY26 no explicit China update — VMware software revenue partially China-insulated vs semi exposure
Semi +52% YoY Q1 beat consensus; management signaled custom AI ASIC backlog extending into FY27; no inventory digestion signals in DC

AVGO.verdict: Thesis intact: AVGO custom AI ASIC acceleration to +52% with stable 68% GM is a structural win; concentration rising to 50% is the key watch — any hyperscaler program delay would have outsized impact.

ANET.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +28.6% to +35.1% while deferred revenue surged +$826M in a single quarter and total RPO hit $7.7B — hyperscaler AI cluster orders are building the backlog faster than ship-through.

shifts.trend: Revenue +28.6% FY25 → +35.1% Q1 FY26; product revenue $7.58B FY25 → $2.71B Q1 ($10.8B annualized)
Deferred revenue: $5.37B FY25 → $6.20B Q1 (+$826M in 90 days); RPO $6.1B → $7.7B (+$1.6B); binding product backlog $968M
shifts.note: Deferred + RPO surge signals order velocity exceeding shipment capacity

shifts.trend: Cloud+AI Titans 48% FY25; Americas share 79.7% → 84.5% Q1 as US hyperscaler build dominates; two customers 26% and 16% FY25
shifts.note: Americas concentration rising signals US AI cluster spend is accelerating

shifts.trend: Product GM 63.7% FY25 → 61.9% Q1 FY26 (-180bps); mix shift toward higher-cost 800G Etherlink platforms compressing near-term margin
shifts.note: Margin dip is platform-transition driven, not demand-driven — watch for recovery

shifts.trend: Etherlink 800G portfolio launched FY25; Q1 FY26 Americas +53% YoY; international flat as AI spend concentrates in US data centers
Q1 FY26 beat consensus on revenue and deferred; $7.7B RPO implies 8+ months of revenue at current run-rate — guide credibility high

ANET.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening: $826M deferred surge in one quarter with +35% revenue growth and $7.7B RPO is the clearest signal that Ethernet AI fabric is taking share — the GM dip on 800G ramp is the only caution flag.

LITE.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +65.5% to +90.1% YoY in one quarter while gross margin expanded +1540bps — Systems (800G transceivers) drove the step-change as OCS crossed $25M in a quarter.

shifts.trend: Revenue: +65.5% FQ2 → +90.1% FQ3; Components +68% → +77%; Systems +60% → +121%; total $665.5M → $808.4M (+21% QoQ)
shifts.note: Systems doubling QoQ growth rate is the most important data point

shifts.trend: GM 36.1% FQ2 (+1130bps YoY) → 44.2% FQ3 (+1540bps YoY); 800bps of sequential expansion in one quarter as volume leverage kicks in
shifts.note: Gross margin expansion pace is exceptional — volume/mix driving structural improvement

shifts.trend: Systems revenue +121% FQ3 YoY — 800G transceiver demand from hyperscaler AI clusters; OCS >$25M quarterly milestone crossed FQ3
shifts.note: OCS crossing $25M is the key milestone for the next-gen optical thesis

shifts.trend: Top-2 customers: 24%+17% FQ2 → 26%+12% FQ3; top customer growing share while #2 declines — hyperscaler concentration increasing
FQ3 beat FQ2 guide on both revenue ($808M vs ~$750M implied) and GM (44.2% vs 38% implied); Systems outperformance driving upside
Both quarters cite strong demand with no inventory digestion signals; backlog building as 800G transceivers face supply constraints

LITE.verdict: Thesis strongly intact: +90% growth with 800bps sequential GM expansion and OCS crossing $25M in one quarter is the fastest optical inflection in the dataset — the only risk is customer concentration rising above 26% for the top hyperscaler.

COHR.headline: Datacom & Communications segment accelerated from +34% to +41% YoY as segment profit grew +44% then +49% — portfolio cleanup (aerospace, Munich divestitures) concentrated the P&L on the winning segment.

shifts.trend: D&C revenue: +34% FQ2 → +41% FQ3; total revenue +17% FQ2 → +21% FQ3; D&C absolute $1.21B → $1.36B (+12% QoQ)
shifts.note: D&C acceleration is 800G transceiver and EML laser driven

shifts.trend: GM 37% FQ2 (+140bps YoY) → 38% FQ3 (+243bps YoY); D&C segment profit +44% FQ2 → +49% FQ3; operating leverage building
shifts.note: GM expanding sequentially as D&C mix grows and Industrial declines

shifts.trend: Industrial segment -10% FQ2 → -16% FQ3; aerospace divested Sep 2025; Munich divested Jan 2026 — portfolio simplification accelerating
shifts.note: Divestitures remove drag — remaining business is D&C focused

shifts.trend: D&C as % of revenue: $1.21B/$1.69B = 72% FQ2 → $1.36B/$1.81B = 75% FQ3; company increasingly a pure-play optical transceiver story
FQ3 revenue $1.81B beat FQ2-implied trajectory; 'scale across' category newly named in FQ3 — management signaling further D&C ramp
No inventory digestion language in either quarter; 800G demand cited as sustained across both filings; Industrial weakness not affecting D&C capacity allocation

COHR.verdict: Thesis intact: D&C at +41% with margin expansion and Industrial/aerospace divestitures complete makes COHR an increasingly clean 800G transceiver play — watch for Industrial decline to trough and D&C margin to reach >40% GM.

CRWV.headline: Revenue grew +112% YoY to $2.08B in Q1 while RPO surged from $60.7B to $98.8B — Microsoft concentration fell from 67% to 45% as Meta emerged as a 20% customer, but $21.6B debt at 9–15% is a structural risk.

shifts.trend: Revenue +168% FY25 → +112% Q1 2026; Q1 annualized $8.3B vs $5.1B FY25; RPO $60.7B → $98.8B (+63% in one quarter)
shifts.note: Deceleration in YoY % on higher base — absolute trajectory remains steep

shifts.trend: Microsoft 67% FY25 → 45% Q1 2026 → 72% FY24 (peak); Meta 20% new customer; customer diversification meaningfully improving in one quarter
shifts.note: Diversification is the most important structural improvement in this filing

shifts.trend: Power 850 MW active FY25 → 3.1 GW contracted; Q1 deferred revenue $7.5B; infrastructure constraints are the binding factor, not demand
shifts.note: 3.1 GW contracted vs 850 MW active signals 3.6x capacity ramp underway

shifts.trend: $21.6B debt at 9–15% FY25; April 2026 raised $4B convert + $2.8B senior + $1B equity ($7.8B total); total debt approaching $29B
shifts.note: High-yield debt at 9–15% on $8B revenue is an existential risk if growth stalls

shifts.trend: JV equity stakes $1.2B Q1 2026; April capital raise $7.8B for infra expansion; capex-to-revenue ratio structurally elevated
RPO $60.7B → $98.8B in one quarter; $7.5B deferred revenue; pipeline building faster than revenue recognition
shifts.note: RPO trajectory at 12x annualized revenue is unprecedented for an infrastructure company

CRWV.verdict: Highest-risk/highest-reward in the dataset: customer diversification improving and RPO doubling are thesis-positive; $29B in high-yield debt funding 3.6x capacity ramp is the thesis-killer scenario if hyperscaler demand shifts even modestly.

LRCX.headline: Memory mix expanded from 34% to 39% of revenue as 9-month revenue grew +24% YoY — DRAM/HBM driven WFE is broadening while China held at ~34–35% without further compression.

shifts.trend: Memory share: 34% Q2 → 39% Q3; Foundry 59% → 54%; Korea +23% QoQ on HBM capacity adds; Memory WFE driven by HBM3/HBM4 capacity
shifts.note: Memory mix shift is the key leading indicator for HBM cycle duration

shifts.trend: 6M revenue $10.67B (+25% YoY) → 9M $16.51B (+24%); Q3 revenue $5.84B (+9% QoQ); consistent mid-20s YoY growth maintained
China 35% Q2 → 34% Q3; no further compression — floor appears established for now; Korea +23% offsetting any China risk
shifts.note: China stabilization removes a key bear-case pressure point

shifts.trend: GM 49.6% Q2 → 49.8% Q3 (+20bps); deferred revenue $2.25B flat QoQ; margin floor holding despite mix shift
Q3 buybacks $1.16B + dividends $326M + $751M debt retirement; total capital return ~$1.5B+ in one quarter on $5.84B revenue
shifts.note: Capital return intensity signals management confidence in cycle durability

shifts.trend: Deferred revenue $2.25B Q2 (flat into Q3) — service/upgrade cycle active; no customer push-out language in either quarter

LRCX.verdict: Thesis intact: Memory mix expanding to 39% with Korea/HBM driving the incremental demand, China floor stabilizing at 34%, and strong capital returns signal a WFE cycle that has legs through at least FY26 — the key risk is a DRAM oversupply event.

KLAC.headline: Korea surged +80% YoY as a region in Q3, offsetting China falling from 30% to 24% of revenue — process control spending at the leading edge is healthy while service revenue grew +16–18% in both quarters.

shifts.trend: Revenue +7% Q2 → +11% Q3; Korea +34% Q2 → +80% Q3 ($565M → $681M); service +18% Q2 → +16% Q3 — recurring revenue growing faster than products
shifts.note: Korea +80% is the signal that Samsung/SK Hynix HBM process control ramp is accelerating

shifts.trend: China $995M (30.2% of revenue, -9% YoY) Q2 → $830M (24%, +5% YoY) Q3; absolute China revenue fell but YoY growth turned positive — stabilization
shifts.note: China turning YoY positive while mix declining is a neutral-to-positive development

shifts.trend: GM 61.4% Q2 → 61.1% Q3 (-30bps); 17th consecutive dividend increase in Q3; operating discipline maintained
shifts.note: 61%+ GM structural floor reflects process control pricing power

shifts.trend: Product revenue +4% Q2 → +10% Q3; service revenue +18% Q2 → +16% Q3; Korea-driven product acceleration validates HBM capacity ramp
17 consecutive dividend increases; consistent buyback program; GM stability at 61% generating strong FCF for returns
Korea 34% → 80% growth pulling share from China 30% → 24%; geographic diversification improving as ally-nation spending dominates

KLAC.verdict: Thesis intact: Korea/HBM process control ramp at +80% validates the memory spending cycle; China stabilizing YoY removes the largest bear case; 61%+ GM and 17 consecutive dividend increases signal structural quality — low volatility compounder in WFE.

VRT.headline: Americas revenue grew +53.1% in Q1 as backlog doubled from $7.2B to $15.0B — gross margin expanded +400bps to 37.7% in one quarter on operating leverage, and net income grew +137%.

shifts.trend: Revenue +27.7% FY25 → +30.1% Q1 FY26; Americas +41.9% FY25 → +53.1% Q1; net income +137% Q1 on operating leverage
shifts.note: Americas acceleration to +53% signals US AI data center thermal management is the fastest-growing vertical

shifts.trend: Backlog $7.2B → $15.0B (doubled in FY25); FY26 backlog trajectory not yet disclosed but 'very robust' demand cited; order velocity maintained
GM 36.3% FY25 → 37.7% Q1 FY26 (+140bps); op profit +51.4% Q1; net income $390M Q1 (+137% YoY) — margin expansion accelerating
shifts.note: 400bps improvement cited in the 10-Q footnotes — volume leverage is real

shifts.trend: FY26 capex guided $425–525M vs $226M FY25 (2x+); Q1 capex $114M on pace with guide; PurgeRite acquisition adds cooling capacity
Q1 FY26 beat on revenue (+30%), margin (+400bps), and net income (+137%); 'very robust AI/HPC demand' language maintained — no demand softening signals
Dividend raised FY25; FY26 capex doubling suggests mgmt prioritizing growth over buybacks — appropriate given backlog trajectory

VRT.verdict: Thesis strongly intact: $15B backlog with +53% Americas growth and +137% net income growth in one quarter positions VRT as the clearest beneficiary of AI data center thermal management spend — capex doubling to fund growth is disciplined not alarming.

ETN.headline: Electrical Americas backlog grew +44% YoY to $14.5B with book-to-bill 1.2x and orders +42% organic — margin dipped 440bps on commodity inflation but the pipeline build is accelerating.

shifts.trend: EA backlog $13.2B FY25 (+31% YoY) → $14.5B Q1 FY26 (+44% YoY); orders +42% organic Q1; book-to-bill 1.2x — still filling faster than shipping
shifts.note: Book-to-bill >1.0 for multiple consecutive quarters signals sustained demand

shifts.trend: EA revenue +16% FY25 → +20% Q1 FY26; Electrical Global backlog +73%; Boyd Thermal $9.5B acquisition adds cooling capacity
shifts.note: EG backlog +73% signals international data center build accelerating

shifts.trend: EA op margin 30.0% FY25 → 25.6% Q1 FY26 (-440bps); commodity inflation cited as primary driver; Q1 margin is cyclical not structural
shifts.note: Margin dip on commodity costs with +20% revenue growth — operating leverage should recover

shifts.trend: Boyd Thermal acquisition $9.5B announced Nov 2025; Fibrebond + Resilient + UltraPCS acquired FY25; M&A pace accelerating to build data center portfolio
Orders +42% organic Q1 with 1.2x book-to-bill; management maintained positive FY26 outlook; no backlog normalization signals
EA dominates portfolio; EG backlog +73% signals geographic diversification improving; no single customer >10% disclosed

ETN.verdict: Thesis intact: EA backlog at $14.5B with 1.2x book-to-bill and +42% organic orders is the strongest demand signal in the electrical infrastructure space; Q1 margin dip on commodities is a timing issue not a structural problem — Boyd Thermal adds scale.

GEV.headline: RPO grew from $150.2B to $163.3B in one quarter as Power EBITDA margin expanded from 11.6% to 16.3% — HA-Turbine orders accelerating and equipment RPO surged $11.7B QoQ.

shifts.trend: Total RPO $150.2B FY25 → $163.3B Q1 2026 (+$13.1B in one quarter, +32% YoY); equipment RPO $64.2B → $75.9B; Power RPO $94.4B FY25 (+29% YoY)
shifts.note: RPO compounding at $13B/quarter signals multi-year demand visibility

shifts.trend: Gas turbine orders 68 FY24 → 110 FY25; HA-Turbines 25 FY24 → 43 FY25; Q1 2026: 12 HA (vs 8 prior year Q1), 28 heavy-duty (vs 29) — HA-Turbine acceleration
shifts.note: HA-Turbine doubling is the critical premium-product signal

shifts.trend: Power EBITDA margin 11.6% prior-year Q1 → 16.3% Q1 2026 (+470bps); FCF $4.8B Q1 2026 vs $1.0B prior year Q1 (4.8x)
shifts.note: FCF 4.8x is exceptional — Power cycle operating leverage materializing

shifts.trend: Prolec GE acquisition $5.3B closed Feb 2026; adds transformer manufacturing to complete the grid interconnect stack; M&A pace accelerating
RPO trajectory $150B → $163B in 90 days with no order slowdown language; HA-Turbine backlog multi-year given 2-3 year lead times
Power RPO $94.4B (+29%); Prolec GE adds transformer capacity; data center gas turbine demand specifically called out in both filings

GEV.verdict: Thesis strongly intact: GEV's HA-Turbine order doubling with 4.8x FCF growth and $163B RPO makes this the clearest infrastructure beneficiary of AI data center power demand — Power margin at 16.3% is still early in the operating leverage curve.

CEG.headline: Calpine closed Jan 7 2026 at $22B, creating a 55 GW combined portfolio; Meta Clinton PPA and Crane DOE $1B guarantee validate the nuclear-for-AI power thesis across three consecutive filings.

shifts.trend: Calpine acquisition $22B closed Jan 7 2026; Crane DOE $1B loan guarantee secured; 55 GW combined capacity vs ~30 GW standalone; NY ZEC extension Jan 2026
shifts.note: Calpine transforms CEG from nuclear pure-play to diversified clean power

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $25.5B; adj EPS $9.39; Q1 2026 is first full quarter with Calpine — revenue step-up expected; Meta PPA added through Q3 2025
shifts.note: Calpine revenue consolidation will dramatically change reported trajectory

shifts.trend: Nuclear fleet + 55 GW combined; data center PPA pipeline building (Meta Clinton Q3 2025); New York ZEC extension secures New York nuclear economics
$22B Calpine acquisition funded with debt; balance sheet leverage significantly increased; Q1 2026 will show first post-acquisition capital structure
shifts.note: Leverage increase is the key risk to monitor post-Calpine

shifts.trend: Adj EPS $9.39 FY25; FY26 guidance not disclosed in available data; Calpine integration synergies will drive FY26 EPS
Meta PPA added; hyperscaler PPA pipeline building; geographic diversification improved with Calpine's gas fleet across multiple regions

CEG.verdict: Thesis intact: Calpine closes the critical gap between CEG's nuclear base-load and the need for dispatchable power — the combination creates the only utility-scale clean power provider capable of meeting hyperscaler 24/7 power demand at scale.

OKLO.headline: Order book reached ~18,100 MWe while Meta signed a 1.2 GW prepayment agreement — NRC Phase I complete with no gaps, but cash burn guided $80–100M in FY26 with $350–450M capex ahead.

shifts.trend: Order book ~18,100 MWe Q3 2025; Meta 1.2 GW prepayment agreement Jan 2026; Tennessee fuel center roadmap $1.68B; Aurora DOE safety approved
shifts.note: Pre-revenue order book at 18 GW is extraordinary relative to zero deployed capacity

shifts.trend: FY26 capex guided $350–450M; Tennessee fuel center $1.68B multi-year; 9M cash burn $48.7M through Q3 2025 → FY26 guided $80–100M
shifts.note: Burn rate doubling signals development phase intensifying

shifts.trend: NRC Phase I: no gaps identified (Q3 2025); Aurora DOE safety review approved (FY25); DOE reactor pilot projects (3 of 11) — regulatory de-risking progressing
shifts.note: Two consecutive regulatory approvals is the most important de-risking signal

shifts.trend: Pre-revenue across all three filings; Q1 2026 10-Q continues development stage; first power expected late-decade; Meta prepayment is first commercial anchor
$968M raised Q3 2025; FY26 cash runway adequate for guided burn; Meta 1.2 GW deal validates commercial model before first unit deployed
Meta 1.2 GW = dominant single customer commitment; hyperscaler concentration is a feature (validation) and risk (dependency) simultaneously

OKLO.verdict: Thesis intact but binary: NRC progression and Meta prepayment de-risk the two largest concerns (regulatory and commercial) — the investment case is a 2028–2030 option on small modular reactor economics, not a near-term earnings story.

MOD.headline: Climate Solutions revenue accelerated from +24% to +51% YoY in one quarter as DC cooling added $130M incremental — the RMT spin of Performance Tech will return $210M cash and sharpen the DC focus.

shifts.trend: Climate Solutions: +24% Q2 → +51% Q3; total revenue +12% Q2 → +31% Q3; CS absolute $454.4M → $544.6M (+20% QoQ); DC contribution +$67M Q2 → +$130M Q3
shifts.note: DC cooling contribution doubling QoQ is the key acceleration signal

shifts.trend: CS margin 13.7% Q2 (-440bps YoY, ramp costs) → 15.3% Q3 (-200bps YoY); margin headwind narrowing as ramp costs absorbed
shifts.note: Margin trajectory improving QoQ despite ramp — recovery path visible

shifts.trend: $100M incremental DC capex planned Q2; spin of Performance Tech returns $210M cash at close; net capex position improving post-spin
DC component of Climate Solutions: +$67M Q2 → +$130M Q3 incremental; Performance Tech spin separates the non-DC segment
shifts.note: Spin concentrates capital on the highest-growth segment

shifts.trend: Q3 beat Q2 implied trajectory by ~$60M on CS revenue; $1B Gentherm merger value + $210M cash return at RMT spin close
RMT spin + Gentherm merger generates ~$1.21B total value ($1B equity + $210M cash); significant capital return event in FY26

MOD.verdict: Thesis intact: CS at +51% with DC contribution doubling QoQ validates the liquid-cooled data center thesis; the RMT spin sharpens the pure-play DC story and returns $210M cash — margin recovery from ramp costs is the near-term catalyst to watch.

CLS.headline: Revenue grew +53% YoY in Q1 as HPS (AI server liquid cooling) hit 42% of revenue at $1.7B — CCS margin expanded 60bps to 8.6% despite a $1B capex year and Enterprise rebounded +101%.

shifts.trend: Revenue: FY25 $12.2B → Q1 FY26 $4.05B (+53% YoY; $16B annualized); CCS +42% FY25 → +76% Q1; HPS +81% FY25 → +63% Q1
shifts.note: Total revenue at +53% with HPS at 42% of mix is an exceptional combination

shifts.trend: HPS (AI server cooling) $5.0B FY25 (41%) → ~$1.7B Q1 (42%); Enterprise +101% Q1 on next-gen program ramp (reversed from -19% FY25 pause)
shifts.note: Enterprise reversal from -19% to +101% is a meaningful positive surprise

shifts.trend: CCS margin 8.0% FY25 → 8.6% Q1 FY26 (+60bps); adj EPS $2.16 beat guide; revolver upsized to $1.75B for growth funding
shifts.note: 60bps margin expansion during a $1B capex year signals operational leverage

shifts.trend: 3 customers >10%: 32%, 14%, 12% FY25; Q1 not broken out but top-3 likely stable; hyperscaler concentration high but known
FY26 capex ~$1B (6% of revenue); revolver upsized $1.75B; capacity investment supports HPS growth trajectory
Q1 adj EPS $2.16 beat; Enterprise +101% reversal signals next-gen program ramp executing; FY26 HPS trajectory appears above FY25 80% growth rate

CLS.verdict: Thesis strongly intact: +53% total growth with HPS at 42% of revenue and Enterprise reversing to +101% leaves virtually every segment contributing — CLS is the clearest liquid cooling EMS play with a credible margin expansion path.

NOW.headline: Subscription revenue held +22% YoY with RPO $27.7B (+25%) and cRPO growing 23% — Now Assist AI agents are deeply embedded but growth acceleration has not materialized yet.

shifts.trend: Revenue +21% FY25 → +22% Q1 2026; subscription +22% in both periods; no acceleration visible despite Now Assist embedding
shifts.note: Growth stability at 22% is solid but AI uplift not yet in numbers

shifts.trend: RPO $28.2B FY25 → $27.7B Q1 2026 (slight decline QoQ); cRPO +25% FY25 → +23% Q1 — marginal deceleration in forward pipeline
shifts.note: cRPO deceleration 25% → 23% is worth monitoring across next two quarters

shifts.trend: Subscription 97% of revenue both periods; federal self-hosted $492M FY25 → $138M Q1 (quarterly, slight decline); professional services +19%
Op margin and subscription economics stable YoY; Now Assist upsell not yet visibly lifting ARPU metrics in disclosed data
Enterprise-wide platform with no single customer >10%; government self-hosted growing as a federal AI workflow layer
FY25 guide met; Q1 2026 in-line; management citing Now Assist deal momentum but quantified uplift not yet in reported metrics
shifts.note: Guidance cadence reliable but not inflecting upward

NOW.verdict: Thesis intact but waiting for the catalyst: NOW is a high-quality compounder at 22% growth with deep AI embedding — the investment case requires Now Assist to lift ARPU and re-accelerate cRPO beyond 25%, which has not happened yet in the filings.

SNOW.headline: Product revenue grew +29–31% YoY with NRR stable at 125% and RPO expanding from $7.9B to $9.8B — SBC compression from 41% to 34% of revenue is the most significant structural improvement.

shifts.trend: Product revenue +29% Q3 FY26 → +31% FY26 full year; customers 13,328 (+21%); $1M+ customers 733 (+27%) — enterprise penetration deepening
RPO $7.9B Q3 → $9.8B FY26 (+24%); backlog replenishment healthy; no consumption slowdown signals
NRR 125% stable in both periods; $1M+ customer growth +27% suggests enterprise expansion healthy; no single customer >10% disclosed
SBC 41% FY25 → 34% FY26 of revenue; cash flow from ops $1.2B FY26; convertible note trigger met Q3 — capital structure improving
shifts.note: SBC compression is the most important structural improvement in the window

shifts.trend: FY26 revenue $4.7B (+31%); NRR held at 125%; no acceleration or deceleration signals in guidance language
Product revenue dominant; professional services minimal; AI/ML workloads cited as consumption growth driver but not yet quantified separately

SNOW.verdict: Thesis intact with improving quality: 125% NRR stability with SBC compressing from 41% to 34% and $1.2B operating cash flow make SNOW a maturing high-growth story — AI workload consumption is the next catalyst but needs quantified evidence in upcoming filings.

CRM.headline: Annual RPO grew to $72.4B (+14%) with cRPO +16% as Agentforce branding replaced Einstein — op margin expanded to 20% while $12.7B in buybacks signal cash generation confidence.

shifts.trend: Revenue +9% Q3 FY26 → +10% FY26 full year; Agentforce revenue not yet separately disclosed; total RPO $59.5B Q3 → $72.4B FY26 (+21% step-up)
shifts.note: RPO step-up from $59.5B to $72.4B in one quarter suggests strong Q4 bookings

shifts.trend: Total RPO $72.4B (+14% YoY); cRPO $35.1B (+16%); Q3 cash $11.3B before FY26 10-K improvement; committed future revenue stable
Op margin 19% FY25 → 20% FY26; $12.7B in buybacks; FCF generation supporting capital returns at scale
Informatica acquisition $9.6B; $12.7B in buybacks; dual capital allocation (M&A + buybacks) at this scale is unusual and signals strong FCF
shifts.note: Informatica adds data management to Agentforce pipeline

shifts.trend: Enterprise-wide platform; no single customer >10%; Agentforce targeting enterprise workflow automation broadly
FY26 10-K in-line with prior guidance; cRPO +16% sets up Q1 FY27; Agentforce branding not yet translating to re-acceleration

CRM.verdict: Thesis intact but pedestrian: CRM at 10% growth with solid RPO and buybacks is a quality business — Agentforce must visibly re-accelerate revenue to +15%+ to unlock multiple expansion; the Informatica acquisition is the key strategic bet to watch.

PLTR.headline: Revenue surged +85% YoY in Q1 2026 as commercial revenue grew +95% and op margin expanded from 20% to 46% — government +76% and contribution margin of 66% validate the operating leverage thesis.

shifts.trend: Revenue +53% FY25 (gov) / +60% (commercial) → Q1 2026 total +85%; commercial +95% Q1; gov +76% Q1; total $1.63B Q1 vs $4.5B FY25 ($6.5B annualized)
shifts.note: Commercial at +95% is the fastest growth in PLTR's history

shifts.trend: Op margin 20% FY25 → 46% Q1 2026 (+2600bps); op income $754M Q1 (+328% YoY); GM 80% FY25 → 87% Q1; contribution margin 66%
shifts.note: Op margin 20% → 46% in one filing period is exceptional operating leverage

shifts.trend: Gov $2.40B FY25 → $858M Q1 (+76%); commercial $2.07B FY25 → $774M Q1 (+95%); US 79% of revenue — domestic AI platform demand dominant
shifts.note: Commercial now nearly matching government — portfolio balance shifting

shifts.trend: US 79% of Q1 revenue; commercial growing faster than government — customer base diversifying away from government concentration
Q1 2026 significantly beat FY25 implied quarterly run-rate; op income $754M Q1 implies $3B annual run-rate — 2x FY25 op income in one quarter
No material buyback or dividend announced; FCF reinvested in growth; contribution margin 66% leaves room for capital return as growth matures

PLTR.verdict: Thesis dramatically strengthened: +85% revenue with 46% op margin and 87% GM in a single quarter is a step-function change — PLTR is no longer a 'future profitability' story, it's demonstrating SaaS-scale economics on government AI deployments right now.

CDNS.headline: Revenue accelerated from +14% to +19% YoY while China reversed from flat to +36% — backlog grew $200M in one quarter and $128.5M BIS settlement was absorbed without GM impact.

shifts.trend: Revenue +14% FY25 → +19% Q1 FY26; China $680M (+19%) FY25 → $189M (+36%) Q1; Japan +36%, Other Asia +23% Q1 — geographic diversification broadening
shifts.note: China re-acceleration to +36% is the largest positive surprise

shifts.trend: China $680M FY25 (flat after BIS whiplash May–Jul 2025) → $189M Q1 FY26 (+36% YoY); BIS disruption absorbed; licensing cadence normalizing
$128.5M BIS settlement paid FY25 → no new export restrictions Q1 FY26; regulatory overhang cleared; backlog $7.8B → $8.0B (+$200M) post-settlement
shifts.note: Settlement resolution is the key de-risking event in this window

shifts.trend: Op margin 28% FY25 (includes $128.5M settlement) → 29% Q1 FY26; recurring revenue 77% of mix; quality of earnings improving
Backlog $7.8B FY25 → $8.0B Q1 FY26 (+$200M); no customer push-out language; AI chip design tool demand intact
Q1 FY26 +19% beat FY25 +14% run-rate; FY26 growth likely above FY25 consensus; China normalization is an unmodeled upside

CDNS.verdict: Thesis strengthened: BIS settlement behind them, China +36%, and revenue re-accelerating to +19% with backlog growth — CDNS is emerging from an overhang-depressed period and the EDA AI chip design cycle thesis is intact.

SNPS.headline: Ansys contributed $885.6M in Q1 FY26 driving reported +66% revenue growth — Design IP declined -6% while Design Automation grew +96%; $3.5B term loan repaid and $118M restructuring continuing.

shifts.trend: Revenue +15% FY25 (ex-Ansys) → +66% Q1 FY26 (Ansys contributed $885.6M); Design Automation +96%; organic ex-Ansys growth rate underlying ~15-20%
shifts.note: Headline +66% is largely Ansys consolidation — organic rate needs tracking

shifts.trend: Design Automation +96% Q1 (Ansys simulation embedded); Design IP -6% Q1 (weak IC design starts); Processor IP sold to GlobalFoundries — portfolio focus sharpening
shifts.note: Design IP weakness is a concern — IC design environment soft

shifts.trend: $13.5B Ansys acquisition debt → $3.5B term loan paid off Q1 FY26; $34.9B purchase price partially offset by asset sales (Processor IP); deleveraging ahead of schedule
shifts.note: Paying down $3.5B in one quarter signals strong FCF post-Ansys close

shifts.trend: China -22% ex-Ansys FY25; Q1 FY26 no explicit China update — BIS restrictions continuing to suppress EDA China revenue
$118.3M restructuring Q1 FY26 (vs $300–350M total guide); Processor IP sold to GlobalFoundries — asset monetization reducing net capex
Backlog $11.4B FY25 → $11.3B Q1 FY26 (stable); restructuring charges suppressing near-term reported margin; underlying improving

SNPS.verdict: Thesis intact but complex: Ansys integration is deleveraging faster than expected ($3.5B repaid Q1) while Design IP weakness signals a soft IC design environment — the combined SNPS+Ansys platform for AI chip design is the medium-term thesis; near-term China and IP drag are the risks.

INTC.headline: DCAI grew +22% YoY to $5.1B in Q1 while Foundry external revenue declined to $174M — 18A ramp to HVM continues but Foundry losses at $2.4B quarterly remain the central drag.

shifts.trend: DCAI: +22% Q1 2026 (vs flat total FY25 revenue -0.5%); CCG +1% Q1; DCAI ASPs +16% on premium mix shift — AI-capable silicon commanding premium
shifts.note: DCAI re-acceleration to +22% with +16% ASP is the most positive signal

shifts.trend: Foundry external revenue $307M FY25 → $174M Q1 2026 (annualized $700M vs $307M — declining run-rate); Foundry operating loss $10.3B FY25 → $2.4B Q1 ($9.6B annualized)
shifts.note: Foundry external revenue declining despite 18A HVM ramp is concerning

shifts.trend: GM 39.4% Q1 2026 (+2.5pts YoY); Q1 restructuring $4.1B (one-time); underlying margin improving as Intel 7 constraints resolved
NVIDIA $5B strategic investment + SoftBank $2B + 159M shares to US Gov — balance sheet restructuring ongoing; capex trajectory unclear pending Foundry strategy review
NVIDIA $5B + SoftBank $2B investments reduce capital burden; US government equity stake signals strategic importance but dilutive
Q1 DCAI +22% beat low expectations; Foundry ramp timeline unchanged; 18A HVM confirmed but customer tapeouts needed to validate external revenue

INTC.verdict: Thesis conditional: DCAI at +22% with +16% ASPs is the first real evidence of Intel's AI server recovery; Foundry at $2.4B quarterly loss with $174M external revenue is still not working — thesis depends on 18A winning external foundry customers in the next 2–4 quarters.

AMKR.headline: Arizona advanced packaging facility on track for H1 2028 manufacturing start with $2.5–3.0B capex committed — AI packaging demand strong with SiP growing as a mix-enriching product.

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $904.6M (13.5% of revenue) → FY26 capex $2.5–3.0B (estimated 35–40% of revenue); Arizona 1.8M sq ft facility manufacturing start H1 2028
shifts.note: Capex tripling in one year is the largest investment cycle in AMKR history

shifts.trend: AI advanced packaging demand cited as strong in both filings; SiP (System-in-Package) growing as AI device integration accelerates
Arizona facility pre-committed with customer anchor agreements; advanced packaging capacity globally constrained — AMKR positioning for 2028 supply gap
AI packaging (HBM integration, chiplet interconnect) growing faster than legacy packaging; SiP mix enriching blended ASPs
No single customer >10% explicitly disclosed; Arizona facility likely anchored by a few key hyperscaler/fabless customers
FY26 capex $2.5–3.0B requires significant debt or equity funding; balance sheet leverage will increase materially through FY28 construction

AMKR.verdict: Thesis intact but long-dated: AMKR's Arizona advanced packaging bet is strategically correct for the AI chiplet era — the 2028 manufacturing start means this is a FY27–28 earnings story; near-term capex intensity will pressure FCF and returns through construction.

HPE.headline: AI server demand (Cray supercomputer line) remained strong across both filings while networking recovered post-inventory — storage recovery adding breadth to the demand recovery.

shifts.trend: AI server (Cray/HPC) demand strong FY25; Q1 FY26 AI server momentum continued; storage recovery Q1 adds second growth vector
AI servers: strong both periods; Networking: recovering post-inventory digestion Q1 FY26; Storage: recovering Q1; three segments moving positive simultaneously
shifts.note: Multi-segment recovery is more durable than single-driver growth

shifts.trend: AI server demand cited as strong with limited inventory digestion language in both filings; Cray HPC backlog elevated
AI server mix typically margin-dilutive vs. traditional server; networking recovery margin-accretive; blended margin trajectory neutral-to-improving
Both filings maintain positive AI demand language; no demand softening signals; Juniper Networks integration adding networking scale
Diversified across enterprise, cloud, and HPC customer base; no hyperscaler concentration risk comparable to pure-play AI vendors

HPE.verdict: Thesis intact: HPE's multi-segment recovery with AI server strength, networking recovering, and storage improving is more balanced than pure-play AI infrastructure names — lower risk but also lower upside vs. single-vector AI plays.

FN.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +35.9% to +39.3% YoY in consecutive quarters as 9-month revenue grew +32.5% — op income grew +52.1% in Q3, outpacing revenue growth and indicating margin expansion.

shifts.note: Consistent acceleration across both quarters with no signs of plateau

shifts.trend: GM 12.2% Q2 → 11.9% Q3 (-30bps); op income +52.1% Q3 outpacing revenue +39.3% — below-the-line leverage improving despite slight GM compression
shifts.note: Op income growing faster than revenue signals OpEx discipline

shifts.trend: 9M revenue $3.33B (+32.5% YoY) vs 6M $2.11B (+28.9%); acceleration building across 9 months — not a one-quarter event
No inventory digestion language; optical transceiver/fiber assembly demand for AI cluster interconnects cited as sustained; customer pull-through uninterrupted
FN manufactures for multiple optical/network OEMs; customer diversification not disclosed but contract manufacturing model implies multiple accounts
Two consecutive quarters of acceleration; Q3 beat Q2 implied run-rate; FY26 full-year trajectory tracking above prior guide

FN.verdict: Thesis intact: FN's optical assembly revenue growing at +39% with op income +52% demonstrates the fiber/optical interconnect demand for AI data center networks is durable and translating to earnings leverage — the 12% GM is the key risk if pricing pressure emerges.

CAT.headline: FY26 guidance maintained at 'low double digit growth' despite $2.2–2.4B tariff cost headwind — Q2 tariff costs ~$700M absorbed, with Power & Energy as 25% of tariff impact.

shifts.trend: 'Low double digit growth' FY26 guidance maintained through FY25 10-K and Q2 2026 10-Q despite tariff headwind; Power & Energy data center segment strong
shifts.note: Guidance maintained through tariff uncertainty signals pricing power and backlog visibility

shifts.trend: $2.2–2.4B tariff costs estimated FY26; Q2 tariff costs ~$700M (on pace for $2.8B annualized vs $2.2B guide); Power & Energy 25% of tariff impact = ~$550M annual headwind
shifts.note: Tariff costs tracking above initial estimate — Q3/Q4 guidance revision risk

shifts.trend: FY26 capex ~$3.5B; $300–350M restructuring; capital allocation stable — no major M&A or capex escalation
Power & Energy (data center gensets/turbines) strongest segment; Construction/Resource slower; AI data center power demand driving P&E outperformance
Tariff costs $2.2–2.4B FY26 vs zero prior year; Q2 alone $700M — global supply chain tariff exposure material and rising
Low double digit growth maintained both filings; Power & Energy demand cited as robust; no guide-down despite tariff escalation

CAT.verdict: Thesis intact but tariff risk is real: CAT's data center Power & Energy demand is genuine but $2.8B annualized tariff run-rate exceeds initial $2.2–2.4B guide — if tariffs persist, margin headwind could force a guide-down in H2 2026.

CMI.headline: Power generation segment driven by data center backup demand across both filings — global backlog elevated and DC standby power described as a multi-year growth driver.

shifts.trend: Power generation segment growing both FY25 and Q1 FY26; data center standby demand cited as primary driver in both periods; backlog elevated consecutively
Power generation outperforming legacy on-highway/industrial segments; data center standby power emerging as the dominant secular demand driver vs. traditional markets
Global backlog elevated FY25 → Q1 FY26; no inventory digestion signals; generator lead times extended cited in both filings
Positive power generation outlook maintained through both filings; data center demand language consistent and not softening
No explicit margin deterioration cited; mix shift toward power generation (typically higher margin) should be margin-accretive
Diversified customer base across multiple industries; data center share growing but not yet dominant in total revenue mix

CMI.verdict: Thesis intact: CMI's power generation segment is a direct beneficiary of AI data center backup power demand; the consistency of demand language across both filings and elevated backlog suggest multi-year visibility — not a one-quarter phenomenon.

HUBB.headline: Grid modernization and data center electrical infrastructure demand are driving backlog growth across both filings — utility solutions and DC investment cited as the two primary secular drivers.

shifts.trend: Grid modernization and DC electrical demand cited as growing in both FY25 and Q1 FY26; backlog growing QoQ; utility solutions and electrical segments both positive
Backlog growing across both filings; no customer push-out language; grid/DC demand outpacing Hubb's current production capacity
Utility solutions (grid modernization) and electrical from DC investment are the two fastest-growing verticals; residential stable
shifts.note: Two secular drivers (grid + DC) are independent and additive

shifts.trend: Consistent margin performance across both filings; volume leverage from backlog conversion should be margin-accretive
Positive demand outlook maintained both periods; grid modernization multi-year visibility based on utility capex cycles
Diversified utility + industrial customer base; no hyperscaler concentration; grid spending driven by regulatory mandates not discretionary budgets

HUBB.verdict: Thesis intact: HUBB benefits from two independent secular drivers (grid modernization + DC electrical) that are early in multi-year investment cycles — lower beta than pure-play AI hardware but highly visible backlog provides downside protection.

PWR.headline: Record backlog maintained across both filings driven by AI data center grid electrification and underground power — revenue +13% FY25 with underground power +47%; 15-year pipeline visibility.

shifts.trend: Record backlog FY25 → Q1 FY26 (sequential backlog building); 15-year pipeline cited; underground power +47% FY25 driving multi-year project commitments
shifts.note: 15-year pipeline is the longest visibility window in the dataset

shifts.trend: Revenue +13% FY25; underground power +47% FY25; Q1 FY26 record backlog with AI DC and grid electrification described as accelerating pipeline
Underground power (DC interconnection to grid) +47% FY25; AI DC and grid electrification cited as primary Q1 FY26 pipeline drivers — both secular and accelerating
Strong margin cited in Q1 FY26; PWR's specialty contracting model typically generates 8–12% op margin at scale; mix toward AI DC likely margin-accretive
Record backlog both periods; 15-year pipeline maintained; AI DC demand additive to existing renewable/grid electrification work
Diversified across utility, data center, industrial customers; no single customer >10% given project-based revenue model

PWR.verdict: Thesis strongly intact: PWR's 15-year pipeline with record backlog and +47% underground power growth is driven by two converging tailwinds (grid electrification + AI DC power interconnection) that are independently large — one of the clearest multi-year visibility stories in the dataset.

GNRC.headline: Commercial and industrial data center standby power is growing across both filings while residential generator demand is recovering — backlog building and C&I mix enriching toward higher-margin DC.

shifts.trend: C&I (data center standby) strong FY25 → growing Q1 FY26; residential recovering Q1 FY26 (from weak FY25); DC standby emerging as the dominant C&I end-market
shifts.note: Two simultaneous tailwinds: DC C&I growing + residential recovering

shifts.trend: C&I DC standby growing both periods; residential recovery adds Q1 FY26; backlog building as lead times extend on DC demand
Backlog building FY25 → Q1 FY26; generator lead times extended on C&I demand; no inventory push-back signals
C&I DC standby typically higher margin than residential; mix shift toward C&I should be margin-accretive across both periods
Positive C&I and residential outlook maintained both periods; DC standby demand described as multi-year given data center build timelines
C&I customer base diversified across DC operators, healthcare, utilities; residential through dealer network; no hyperscaler concentration

GNRC.verdict: Thesis intact: GNRC's C&I data center standby demand with residential recovery is a balanced and building story — less pure-play than CMI but residential recovery adds upside optionality; backlog building confirms the DC standby power thesis is real.

NVT.headline: Data center is the fastest-growing vertical across both filings with enclosures and thermal management demand accelerating — strong DC thermal management growth continuing into Q1 FY26.

shifts.trend: DC thermal management cited as fastest-growing vertical FY25; strong continuation Q1 FY26; enclosures and thermal growing in both periods
DC enclosures and thermal management outperforming legacy industrial/commercial segments; AI GPU density driving liquid cooling retrofit demand
shifts.note: AI GPU density is driving a retrofit cycle in NVT's DC thermal products

shifts.trend: DC thermal demand strong with no inventory digestion signals; backlog building on liquid cooling product lines
DC thermal and enclosures typically higher margin than legacy segments; mix shift should be margin-accretive
Positive DC demand language maintained both filings; thermal management described as a multi-year growth driver given AI infrastructure build timeline
Diversified across hyperscalers, co-location providers, and enterprise DC operators; no single customer concentration risk

NVT.verdict: Thesis intact: NVT's DC thermal and enclosure growth across both filings is driven by the GPU density-to-liquid cooling retrofit cycle — a less volatile entry point into AI infrastructure spending than semiconductor or networking plays.

LEU.headline: Long-term SWU contracts are locking in nuclear fuel supply for the data center power renaissance across both filings — data center nuclear demand is now explicitly cited as a demand driver in both periods.

shifts.trend: Long-term SWU contracts locked FY25; additional contracts growing Q1 2026; nuclear enrichment capacity is globally constrained with US domestic capacity limited
shifts.note: Long-term contracts signal utilities are making decade-long fuel commitments

shifts.trend: SWU contracts growing Q1 2026; data center nuclear power demand cited as explicit growth driver in both periods; nuclear power renaissance driving utility commitment acceleration
Nuclear fuel enrichment (SWU) is the primary product; demand from data center-adjacent nuclear PPAs and utility recommitments both growing
Utility customer base diversified; no single data center operator concentration; revenue driven by SWU contract portfolio
Contract visibility multi-year given enrichment lead times; data center nuclear demand cited as additive to existing utility renewal cycle
US domestic enrichment capacity supported by policy initiatives; LEU positioned as domestic supply security asset amid Russian SWU sanctions
shifts.note: Policy tailwind from domestic enrichment security is a durable structural support

LEU.verdict: Thesis intact: LEU's long-term SWU contract lock-in with data center nuclear demand as an explicit growth driver positions it as a direct beneficiary of the nuclear power renaissance — multi-year contract visibility reduces revenue risk; the key watch is Russian SWU sanction enforcement pace.

MXL.headline: Revenue bottomed in FY25 and coherent DSP is recovering in Q1 2026 — AI datacenter connectivity growing as infrastructure end markets stabilize from the 2023–24 digestion trough.

shifts.trend: Revenue bottomed FY25; Q1 2026 recovery in coherent DSP; AI datacenter connectivity growing; infrastructure end market stabilizing after 2-year digestion
Coherent DSP recovering Q1 2026 (from -60%+ trough in 2024); AI datacenter connectivity (PAM4 DSP) growing as a new demand vector; two drivers simultaneously recovering
shifts.note: Coherent and PAM4 both recovering is a broadening thesis

shifts.trend: Infrastructure end market stabilizing Q1 2026; no further inventory digestion signals; customer pull-through normalizing
Revenue recovery from trough should drive gross margin expansion as fixed costs spread over higher volumes; GM trajectory positive from depressed FY25 base
Recovery language consistent across both filings; management not guiding above recovery pace; AI datacenter connectivity cited as incremental opportunity
Telecom and datacenter customer base; recovery broad-based; no single customer concentration risk flagged in either filing

MXL.verdict: Thesis intact but early: MXL's coherent DSP recovery from a deep trough plus AI datacenter connectivity as a new growth vector is a classic trough-to-recovery play — the investment case requires coherent recovery to sustain and AI PAM4 to scale; FY26 is the validation year.
